retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Posterior Longitudinal Ligament -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: In the anterior surgical decompression, the ossified lesion is removed from the anterior side of the spine. Technically, this surgical procedure is difficult because of the anterior location of the posterior longitudinal ligament in the vertebral canal. Thus, posterior decompression is the choice of surgical procedure in treating the ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament. Although posterior decompression is the surgical procedure of choice, complications may arise. Poor surgical outcomes are due to the following reasons. [17] Physiological kyphosis in the thoracic region The thoracic region of the spinal cord is the watershed region, predisposing to ischemic change.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Degenerative Disk Disease -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lumbar discectomy with fusion: The Swedish Lumbar Spine Study Group has reported systematic evidence on the superiority of outcomes of fusion for degenerative lumbar disc disease when compared with nonsurgical treatment. [12] Fusion has been considered for a long time as the gold standard for the management of lumbar degenerative disc disease. [13] Lumbar total disc replacement: [14] [15] The main indication is a single-level disc disease with disease-free facet joints. In comparison with fusion, this has been reported to have better patient outcomes and lower rates of adjacent segment disease. On the other side, postoperatively, patients may still complain of persistent back pain, which could be due to the subtle instability of the prosthesis. Dislocation of the polyethylene inlay has been reported as a complication as well. This can be treated by either revision arthroplasty or conversion to arthrodesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Degenerative Disk Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: In regards to microdiscectomy postoperative rehabilitation, one study showed superior results when neuromuscular exercise programs were started 2 weeks post-surgery (compared to those starting at the traditional 6-week mark). Furthermore, at 4 to 6 weeks postoperatively, evidence shows that intensive exercise programs result in a more rapid short-term improvement of function as well as a return to work when compared to mild intensity programs. Most importantly, these studies show that the more aggressive and sooner-onset therapies did not alter the rates of re-herniation or reoperation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lumbar Degenerative Disk Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of patients who are treated conservatively do have a good outcome, provided they change their lifestyle. For those who undergo surgery, the outcomes do vary from poor to fair. In fact, poor results are universal. Many outcome studies have been misrepresented because they have been published by the surgeon or the orthopedic manufacturer, or sponsorer. The long-term reduction in radicular pain is seen in some patients, but the numbers vary from 30-60%. For those who do not undertake changes in lifestyle, the recurrence of symptoms is very common. [20] [21] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Disk Herniation -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In one study, cartilaginous endplates in herniated disks were evaluated and compared with clinical symptoms and postoperative outcomes. The cellular and tissue composition of herniated disks varied across different age groups and correlated with the severity of symptoms. CE abnormalities were observed with increasing age and were more frequently detected in patients with Modic changes on imaging studies ( P < .001). A higher proportion of lumbar disk herniation with a 20% or greater occupancy rate (eg, CEs with a greater content of water and other substances) was found in patients younger than 50 ( P = .009). These changes were associated with decreased motor strength preoperatively ( P = .007). The postoperative visual analog scale score for low back pain was higher in patients with CE abnormalities than those without in patients older than 50 ( P < .001). In multiple regression analysis, CE abnormalities were independently associated with residual low back pain at 1 year postoperatively in older patients (β= .46, P < .001). The study also stated that avulsion-type herniations in patients younger than 50 had a higher CE occupancy rate, which was a potential cause of preoperative motor weakness. Clinical outcomes following diskectomy improved regardless of the presence of CE abnormalities; however, cartilaginous herniation in patients aged 50 or older may affect residual low back pain at 1 year. [19]

retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacologic management of the ossification of the longitudinal ligament is still not established. The standard treatment is surgical decompression of the spinal cord. Surgical management is done either by anterior decompression or by posterior decompression in modern medical practice. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Degenerative Disc Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Operative Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Recently, cervical disc arthroplasty has gained popularity. Some study results show no significant difference between fusion and arthroplasty regarding revision rates 2 years postoperatively. [53] However, this procedure is not indicated for DDD but rather for herniated discs. Spinal instability is a contraindication for arthroplasty and laminoplasty. A combined approach (anterior and posterior) may be needed when a multi-level corpectomy is indicated or when there is diminished bone quality secondary to the patient’s metabolic state.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Lumbar Spine -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical approaches for interbody fusion are multiple (posterior, transforaminal, oblique, lateral, and anterior). Anterior spinal fusion avoids the canal and nerve roots but does introduce complications such as vascular injury and incisional hernia. There is limited comparative data that one approach is superior to another. Following fusion, chronic postoperative complications include pseudoarthrosis (failure of fusion) and adjacent segment degenerative disc disease above/below the fusion level. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc procedures are associated with the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية). Post‑laminectomy syndrome is classified as a subtype of spine disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري). This relationship highlights that patients undergoing laminectomy or disc excision may experience persistent pain and functional impairment characteristic of this spinal disorder, underscoring the need for careful postoperative management and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1708

Nerve‑root specific exploration is captured by “Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy), with decompression of nerve root(s), including partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of herniated intervertebral disc, reexploration, single interspace; cervical,” and its lumbar counterpart, both identified as explicit methods. All of these procedures are organized under the broader classification “Exploration procedure,” which itself is a child of the parent category “Clinical actions A - G.” This hierarchical structure highlights that each named surgery serves as a distinct methodological approach within the overall clinical framework for diagnostic and therapeutic exploration across endocrine, biliary, cranial, and spinal systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 743
retrieved_docs.content: procedural descriptions. Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) procedures enable nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated disc material, and are specified for single‑level cervical or lumbar re‑exploration. Finally, parathyroidectomy can be performed with mediastinal exploration via a transthoracic approach. Each of these methods represents a specific “has_method” or “has_possibly_included_method” relationship within the overarching exploration procedure framework, highlighting the versatility and anatomical breadth of exploratory surgical techniques in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: Laminectomy and intervertebral disc excision are spinal surgical procedures performed to relieve compression of neural elements and to treat disc pathology. These interventions are directly linked to the development of post‑laminectomy syndrome (متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية), a condition in which patients continue to experience persistent back pain, radicular symptoms, or functional limitation after the surgery. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians, as it underscores the need for careful patient selection, thorough pre‑operative counseling, and postoperative strategies—such as multimodal pain management, rehabilitation, and, when appropriate, additional interventions—to mitigate the risk and impact of post‑laminectomy syndrome on patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 983
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” functions as a parent category that includes spondylosis, intervertebral disc disorders, and other back problems. These specific spinal and disc pathologies are therefore understood as manifestations of the broader bone‑centered disease entity, allowing clinicians to classify and address them within a unified diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: The grouping “spondylosis; intervertebral disc disorders; other back problems” functions as a parent category for the condition referred to as اضطراب العمود الفقري (spinal disorder). Within the broader category of spinal disorders, there is a specific child condition called متلازمة ما بعد استئصال الصفيحة الفقرية (post‑laminectomy syndrome), which represents a subset of spinal problems that can develop after surgical removal of a vertebral lamina. Recognizing that post‑laminectomy syndrome is nested under the general spinal disorder category, which itself falls under the larger umbrella of spondylosis, disc disease, and other back problems, helps clinicians appreciate the hierarchical relationship among these back‑related conditions and supports more precise diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.content: Craniectomy performed suboccipitally for the purpose of exploring or decompressing cranial nerves is also classified as an exploration method. A laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and/or excision of a herniated disc at a single lumbar interspace is specifically listed as a method. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 981
retrieved_docs.content: Post‑laminectomiesyndroom, niet elders geclassificeerd is categorized as a form of Spondylopathies/spondyloarthropathy (including infective), indicating that it belongs to the broader group of spinal joint disorders. This syndrome is directly linked to procedures such as laminectomy and excision of intervertebral disc, reflecting its nature as a postoperative condition that can arise after these specific spinal surgeries. Recognizing its classification and surgical association aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and managing patients who develop persistent symptoms following laminectomy or disc excision.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2448
retrieved_docs.content: Post‑laminectomiesyndroom, niet elders geclassificeerd, is a postoperative condition that occurs after the surgical procedures of laminectomy and excision of an intervertebral disc. The syndrome is characterized by persistent back or radicular pain that continues despite the completion of the laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc surgery, indicating a direct relationship between the condition and these specific spine operations. Recognizing this connection is essential for clinicians to evaluate, monitor, and manage patients who develop chronic spinal pain following such procedures, thereby improving postoperative care and outcomes.

Post‑laminectomy syndrome, not elsewhere classified (Post‑laminectomiesyndroom, niet elders geclassificeerd), is classified as a form of spondylopathies/spondyloarthropathy (including infective) and is directly related to procedures such as laminectomy and excision of intervertebral disc. This syndrome is also linked to the procedural category laminectomy; excision intervertebral disc, reinforcing its origin in spinal surgery. Spondylopathies/spondyloarthropathy (including infective) classifies a group of conditions described as spondylosis; intervertebral disc disorders; other back problems, which are placed under the parent categories spinal disorder (اضطراب العمود الفقري) and bone‑centered pathology (اعتلال مرتكز العظم). Consequently, spinal disorder includes post‑laminectomy syndrome

retrieved_docs.content: includes both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, as well as suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration is frequently achieved through laminectomy, which removes the lamina to allow direct visualization and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; this technique is applied across cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral levels for one or two vertebral segments, and may be extended to more than two segments in the lumbar and thoracic regions. When nerve‑root decompression is required, laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with partial facetectomy and foraminotomy can be performed at a single cervical interspace to excise herniated disc material and re‑explore the area.
